BST (Binary Search Tree)

Overview

Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree data structure where each node follows the ordering property: left child < parent < right child. This property enables efficient searching, insertion, and deletion operations.

Key Properties

  • Time Complexity:
    • Search/Insert/Delete: O(log n) average, O(n) worst case (unbalanced)
    • Traversal: O(n) for all nodes
  • Space Complexity: O(n) for storing n nodes, O(h) for recursive operations
  • Core Property: left < root < right for all nodes
  • Inorder Traversal: Produces sorted sequence (ascending order)
  • When to Use: Sorted data operations, range queries, ordered statistics

References

  • BST Visualizer
  • fucking-algorithm - BST pt.1
  • fucking-algorithm - BST pt.2
  • fucking-algorithm - BST pt.3
1// below will print BST elements in ascending ordering
2// java
3void traverse(TreeNode root) {
4    if (root == null) return;
5    traverse(root.left);
6    // in-order traversal
7    print(root.val);
8    traverse(root.right);

Problem Categories

Pattern 1: BST Search & Validation

  • Description: Find elements or validate BST properties
  • Recognition: “Search”, “find”, “validate”, “is valid BST”
  • Examples: LC 98, LC 700, LC 270, LC 285
  • Template: Use Search Template with BST property

Pattern 2: BST Insertion & Deletion

  • Description: Modify BST structure while maintaining properties
  • Recognition: “Insert”, “delete”, “remove”, “add node”
  • Examples: LC 450, LC 701, LC 669
  • Template: Use Modification Template

Pattern 3: BST Traversal & Conversion

  • Description: Use inorder property for sorting/conversion
  • Recognition: “Kth smallest”, “convert”, “flatten”, “sorted order”
  • Examples: LC 230, LC 173, LC 426, LC 538
  • Template: Use Inorder Template

Pattern 4: BST Construction

  • Description: Build BST from various inputs or rebuild/balance existing BST
  • Recognition: “Construct”, “build”, “generate”, “serialize”, “balance”
  • Examples: LC 108, LC 109, LC 95, LC 96, LC 449, LC 1008, LC 1382
  • Template: Use Construction Template

Pattern 5: BST Properties & Optimization

  • Description: Find optimal values or properties in BST
  • Recognition: “Closest”, “LCA”, “range”, “distance”
  • Examples: LC 235, LC 530, LC 783, LC 776
  • Template: Use Property Template

Pattern 6: Path Problems

  • Description: Problems involving root-to-leaf or node-to-node paths
  • Recognition: “Path sum”, “root to leaf”, “maximum path”, “consecutive sequence”
  • Examples: LC 112, LC 113, LC 257, LC 124, LC 129, LC 298, LC 437
  • Template: Use DFS with path tracking, backtracking, or global state

Templates & Algorithms

Template Comparison Table

Template Type Use Case Key Operation Time Space When to Use
Search Template Finding values Binary search O(log n) O(1)/O(h) Value lookup
Insertion Template Adding nodes Find position + insert O(log n) O(1)/O(h) Adding new values
Deletion Template Removing nodes Find + restructure O(log n) O(h) Removing values
Inorder Template Sorted operations Left-root-right O(n) O(h) Kth element, range
Construction Template Building BST Divide & conquer O(n) O(n) Creating from array
Path Template Root-to-leaf paths DFS + tracking O(n) O(h) Path sum, sequences

Universal BST Template

1def bst_operation(root, target):
2    """
3    Universal template for BST operations
4    Leverages left < root < right property
5    """
6    if not root:
7        return None  # or appropriate base value
8    
9    # BST property for efficient search
10    if target == root.val:
11        # Process current node
12        return root
13    elif target < root.val:
14        # Go left for smaller values
15        return bst_operation(root.left, target)
16    else:
17        # Go right for larger values
18        return bst_operation(root.right, target)

Template 1: BST Search

1def search_bst(root, val):
2    """
3    Search for a value in BST
4    Time: O(log n) average, O(n) worst
5    """
6    if not root or root.val == val:
7        return root
8    
9    if val < root.val:
10        return search_bst(root.left, val)
11    return search_bst(root.right, val)
12
13# Iterative version
14def search_bst_iterative(root, val):
15    while root and root.val != val:
16        root = root.left if val < root.val else root.right
17    return root

Template 2: BST Insertion

1def insert_bst(root, val):
2    """
3    Insert value into BST
4    Always inserts as a leaf node
5    """
6    if not root:
7        return TreeNode(val)
8    
9    if val < root.val:
10        root.left = insert_bst(root.left, val)
11    elif val > root.val:
12        root.right = insert_bst(root.right, val)
13    # If val == root.val, typically don't insert duplicates
14    
15    return root

Template 3: BST Deletion

1def delete_bst(root, key):
2    """
3    Delete a node from BST
4    Three cases: no child, one child, two children
5    """
6    if not root:
7        return None
8    
9    if key < root.val:
10        root.left = delete_bst(root.left, key)
11    elif key > root.val:
12        root.right = delete_bst(root.right, key)
13    else:
14        # Node found - handle 3 cases
15        if not root.left:  # No left child or leaf
16            return root.right
17        if not root.right:  # No right child
18            return root.left
19        
20        # Two children: find inorder successor
21        min_node = find_min(root.right)
22        root.val = min_node.val
23        root.right = delete_bst(root.right, min_node.val)
24    
25    return root
26
27def find_min(node):
28    """Find minimum value in BST (leftmost node)"""
29    while node.left:
30        node = node.left
31    return node

Template 4: BST Validation

1def validate_bst(root):
2    """
3    Validate if tree is a valid BST
4    Uses min/max bounds approach
5    """
6    def validate(node, min_val, max_val):
7        if not node:
8            return True
9        
10        if node.val <= min_val or node.val >= max_val:
11            return False
12        
13        return (validate(node.left, min_val, node.val) and
14                validate(node.right, node.val, max_val))
15    
16    return validate(root, float('-inf'), float('inf'))

Template 5: BST Inorder Operations

1def kth_smallest(root, k):
2    """
3    Find kth smallest element using inorder property
4    Inorder traversal of BST gives sorted order
5    """
6    def inorder(node):
7        if not node:
8            return []
9        return inorder(node.left) + [node.val] + inorder(node.right)
10    
11    return inorder(root)[k-1]
12
13# Optimized with early stopping
14def kth_smallest_optimized(root, k):
15    stack = []
16    while True:
17        while root:
18            stack.append(root)
19            root = root.left
20        root = stack.pop()
21        k -= 1
22        if k == 0:
23            return root.val
24        root = root.right

Template 6: BST Construction

Pattern Overview

  • Description: Build BST from various inputs (arrays, lists, traversals)
  • Recognition: “Construct”, “build”, “generate”, “serialize”, “from preorder/inorder”
  • Key Concept: Use recursive divide-and-conquer with BST property
  • Time Complexity: O(n) for most constructions, O(n log n) for some
  • Space Complexity: O(n) for tree storage + O(h) for recursion stack

Core Construction Patterns

Pattern 6.1: From Sorted Array (LC 108)
1def sorted_array_to_bst(nums):
2    """
3    Convert sorted array to balanced BST
4    Uses binary search approach - pick middle as root
5    Time: O(n), Space: O(n)
6    """
7    if not nums:
8        return None
9
10    mid = len(nums) // 2
11    root = TreeNode(nums[mid])
12    root.left = sorted_array_to_bst(nums[:mid])
13    root.right = sorted_array_to_bst(nums[mid+1:])
14
15    return root
16
17# Optimized version with index (no array slicing)
18def sorted_array_to_bst_optimized(nums):
19    def build(left, right):
20        if left > right:
21            return None
22
23        mid = (left + right) // 2
24        root = TreeNode(nums[mid])
25        root.left = build(left, mid - 1)
26        root.right = build(mid + 1, right)
27        return root
28
29    return build(0, len(nums) - 1)
Pattern 6.2: From Sorted Linked List (LC 109)
1def sorted_list_to_bst(head):
2    """
3    Convert sorted linked list to balanced BST
4    Two approaches: 1) Two pointers to find middle
5                   2) Inorder simulation
6    Time: O(n log n) or O(n), Space: O(log n)
7    """
8    # Approach 1: Find middle with slow-fast pointers
9    def find_middle(left, right):
10        slow = fast = left
11        while fast != right and fast.next != right:
12            slow = slow.next
13            fast = fast.next.next
14        return slow
15
16    def convert(left, right):
17        if left == right:
18            return None
19
20        mid = find_middle(left, right)
21        root = TreeNode(mid.val)
22        root.left = convert(left, mid)
23        root.right = convert(mid.next, right)
24        return root
25
26    return convert(head, None)
Pattern 6.3: From Preorder Traversal (LC 1008)
1def bst_from_preorder(preorder):
2    """
3    Construct BST from preorder traversal
4    Use BST property: left < root < right
5    Time: O(n), Space: O(h)
6    """
7    def build(min_val, max_val):
8        nonlocal idx
9        if idx >= len(preorder):
10            return None
11
12        val = preorder[idx]
13        if val < min_val or val > max_val:
14            return None
15
16        idx += 1
17        root = TreeNode(val)
18        root.left = build(min_val, val)
19        root.right = build(val, max_val)
20        return root
21
22    idx = 0
23    return build(float('-inf'), float('inf'))
Pattern 6.4: Balance a BST (LC 1382)
1def balance_bst(root):
2    """
3    Balance an existing BST by rebuilding it
4    Approach: Inorder traversal + rebuild from sorted nodes
5    Time: O(n), Space: O(n)
6
7    Key Steps:
8    1. Inorder traversal to get sorted node list
9    2. Use middle element as root (like sorted array to BST)
10    3. Recursively build left and right subtrees
11    """
12    # Step 1: Get sorted nodes via inorder traversal
13    nodes = []
14    def inorder(node):
15        if not node:
16            return
17        inorder(node.left)
18        nodes.append(node)
19        inorder(node.right)
20
21    inorder(root)
22
23    # Step 2: Rebuild balanced BST from sorted nodes
24    def build_balanced(left, right):
25        if left > right:
26            return None
27
28        # Pick middle as root to ensure balance
29        mid = (left + right) // 2
30        root = nodes[mid]
31
32        # Recursively build left and right subtrees
33        root.left = build_balanced(left, mid - 1)
34        root.right = build_balanced(mid + 1, right)
35
36        return root
37
38    return build_balanced(0, len(nodes) - 1)

1// Java implementation
2public TreeNode balanceBST(TreeNode root) {
3    List<TreeNode> nodes = new ArrayList<>();
4
5    // Step 1: Inorder traversal to get sorted nodes
6    inorderTraversal(root, nodes);
7
8    // Step 2: Rebuild balanced BST
9    return buildBalancedBST(nodes, 0, nodes.size() - 1);
10}
11
12private void inorderTraversal(TreeNode root, List<TreeNode> nodes) {
13    if (root == null) {
14        return;
15    }
16    inorderTraversal(root.left, nodes);
17    nodes.add(root);
18    inorderTraversal(root.right, nodes);
19}
20
21private TreeNode buildBalancedBST(List<TreeNode> nodes, int left, int right) {
22    if (left > right) {
23        return null;
24    }
25
26    // Pick middle as root for balance
27    int mid = (left + right) / 2;
28    TreeNode root = nodes.get(mid);
29
30    // Recursively build subtrees
31    root.left = buildBalancedBST(nodes, left, mid - 1);
32    root.right = buildBalancedBST(nodes, mid + 1, right);
33
34    return root;
35}
Pattern 6.5: Generate All Unique BSTs (Recursive Construction via Cartesian Product) (LC 95)

Core Idea:

  • Pick each number i in [start, end] as the root
  • All numbers [start, i-1] must form the left subtree (BST property)
  • All numbers [i+1, end] must form the right subtree (BST property)
  • The total unique trees for root i = Cartesian product of all left subtrees × all right subtrees
  • Base case: when start > end, return [null] (empty subtree, NOT empty list)
  • Total count follows Catalan number: C(n) = (2n)! / ((n+1)! × n!)

Approaches:

  1. Plain Recursion — enumerate all combinations directly (overlapping subproblems)
  2. Memoized Recursion — cache (start, end) → List<TreeNode> to avoid recomputation
  3. Iterative DP — bottom-up table dp[start][end] filled by increasing window size
  4. Space-Optimized DP — dp[numberOfNodes] with clone + offset for right subtrees
1def generate_trees(n):
2    """
3    Generate all structurally unique BSTs with n nodes
4    Catalan number of trees: C(n) = (2n)! / ((n+1)! * n!)
5    Time: O(4^n / n^(3/2)), Space: O(4^n / n^(3/2))
6    """
7    if n == 0:
8        return []
9
10    def generate(start, end):
11        if start > end:
12            return [None]
13
14        all_trees = []
15        for root_val in range(start, end + 1):
16            left_trees = generate(start, root_val - 1)
17            right_trees = generate(root_val + 1, end)
18
19            for left in left_trees:
20                for right in right_trees:
21                    root = TreeNode(root_val)
22                    root.left = left
23                    root.right = right
24                    all_trees.append(root)
25
26        return all_trees
27
28    return generate(1, n)

1// Java — Plain Recursion (LC 95)
2public List<TreeNode> generateTrees(int n) {
3    if (n == 0) return new ArrayList<>();
4    return buildTrees(1, n);
5}
6
7private List<TreeNode> buildTrees(int start, int end) {
8    List<TreeNode> allTrees = new ArrayList<>();
9    if (start > end) {
10        allTrees.add(null);  // important: null represents empty subtree
11        return allTrees;
12    }
13    for (int i = start; i <= end; i++) {
14        // left subtree candidates: [start, i-1]
15        List<TreeNode> leftSubtrees = buildTrees(start, i - 1);
16        // right subtree candidates: [i+1, end]
17        List<TreeNode> rightSubtrees = buildTrees(i + 1, end);
18        // Cartesian product: connect each left × right to root i
19        for (TreeNode left : leftSubtrees) {
20            for (TreeNode right : rightSubtrees) {
21                TreeNode root = new TreeNode(i);
22                root.left = left;
23                root.right = right;
24                allTrees.add(root);
25            }
26        }
27    }
28    return allTrees;
29}

1// Java — Memoized Recursion (LC 95)
2public List<TreeNode> generateTrees_memo(int n) {
3    Map<Pair<Integer, Integer>, List<TreeNode>> memo = new HashMap<>();
4    return allPossibleBST(1, n, memo);
5}
6
7private List<TreeNode> allPossibleBST(int start, int end,
8        Map<Pair<Integer, Integer>, List<TreeNode>> memo) {
9    List<TreeNode> res = new ArrayList<>();
10    if (start > end) { res.add(null); return res; }
11    if (memo.containsKey(new Pair<>(start, end)))
12        return memo.get(new Pair<>(start, end));
13    for (int i = start; i <= end; ++i) {
14        List<TreeNode> leftSubs = allPossibleBST(start, i - 1, memo);
15        List<TreeNode> rightSubs = allPossibleBST(i + 1, end, memo);
16        for (TreeNode left : leftSubs)
17            for (TreeNode right : rightSubs)
18                res.add(new TreeNode(i, left, right));
19    }
20    memo.put(new Pair<>(start, end), res);
21    return res;
22}

Similar LeetCode Problems:

  • LC 95: Unique Binary Search Trees II (generate all)
  • LC 96: Unique Binary Search Trees (count only — Catalan number DP)
  • LC 241: Different Ways to Add Parentheses (same Cartesian product pattern)
  • LC 894: All Possible Full Binary Trees
  • LC 1382: Balance a Binary Search Tree
Pattern 6.6: Count Unique BSTs (LC 96)
1def num_trees(n):
2    """
3    Count number of structurally unique BSTs with n nodes
4    Uses Catalan Number: C(n) = C(0)*C(n-1) + C(1)*C(n-2) + ... + C(n-1)*C(0)
5    Time: O(n^2), Space: O(n)
6    """
7    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
8    dp[0] = dp[1] = 1
9
10    for nodes in range(2, n + 1):
11        for root in range(1, nodes + 1):
12            left = root - 1
13            right = nodes - root
14            dp[nodes] += dp[left] * dp[right]
15
16    return dp[n]

Java Implementations

1// Pattern 6.1: From Sorted Array (LC 108)
2public TreeNode sortedArrayToBST(int[] nums) {
3    return buildBST(nums, 0, nums.length - 1);
4}
5
6private TreeNode buildBST(int[] nums, int left, int right) {
7    if (left > right) return null;
8
9    int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
10    TreeNode root = new TreeNode(nums[mid]);
11    root.left = buildBST(nums, left, mid - 1);
12    root.right = buildBST(nums, mid + 1, right);
13    return root;
14}
15
16// Pattern 6.3: From Preorder (LC 1008)
17private int idx = 0;
18
19public TreeNode bstFromPreorder(int[] preorder) {
20    return build(preorder, Integer.MIN_VALUE, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
21}
22
23private TreeNode build(int[] preorder, int min, int max) {
24    if (idx >= preorder.length) return null;
25
26    int val = preorder[idx];
27    if (val < min || val > max) return null;
28
29    idx++;
30    TreeNode root = new TreeNode(val);
31    root.left = build(preorder, min, val);
32    root.right = build(preorder, val, max);
33    return root;
34}

Construction Pattern Summary Table

Input Type Approach Key Technique Time Space LC #
Sorted Array Binary search Pick mid as root O(n) O(n) 108
Sorted List Two pointers Find mid node O(n log n) O(log n) 109
Preorder Bounds checking Use min/max O(n) O(h) 1008
Balance BST Inorder + Rebuild Collect sorted nodes O(n) O(n) 1382
Generate All Combinatorial Try each as root O(4^n/n^1.5) O(4^n/n^1.5) 95
Count Unique Dynamic programming Catalan numbers O(n²) O(n) 96
Serialize Preorder encoding BST property O(n) O(n) 449

Template 7: Path Problems

Pattern Overview

  • Description: Find or validate paths in binary trees (root-to-leaf, node-to-node)
  • Recognition: “Path sum”, “root to leaf”, “maximum path”, “consecutive”
  • Key Concept: Use DFS with path tracking, accumulation, or global state
  • Time Complexity: O(n) for visiting all nodes
  • Space Complexity: O(h) for recursion stack + path storage

📚 Related Patterns: These path problems use DFS traversal. For general tree path-finding patterns and techniques, see dfs.md Template 3 (Path Finding). The examples here focus on common path problems that work for both BST and general binary trees.

Core Path Patterns

Pattern 7.1: Simple Path Sum (LC 112)
1def has_path_sum(root, target_sum):
2    """
3    Check if root-to-leaf path exists with given sum
4    Time: O(n), Space: O(h)
5    """
6    if not root:
7        return False
8
9    # Leaf node check
10    if not root.left and not root.right:
11        return root.val == target_sum
12
13    # Recurse with reduced sum
14    remaining = target_sum - root.val
15    return (has_path_sum(root.left, remaining) or
16            has_path_sum(root.right, remaining))
Pattern 7.2: Path Sum II - All Paths (LC 113)
1def path_sum(root, target_sum):
2    """
3    Find all root-to-leaf paths with given sum
4    Uses backtracking to track current path
5    Time: O(n), Space: O(h)
6    """
7    result = []
8
9    def dfs(node, remaining, path):
10        if not node:
11            return
12
13        # Add current node to path
14        path.append(node.val)
15
16        # Check if leaf with target sum
17        if not node.left and not node.right and remaining == node.val:
18            result.append(path[:])  # Deep copy
19
20        # Recurse on children
21        new_remaining = remaining - node.val
22        dfs(node.left, new_remaining, path)
23        dfs(node.right, new_remaining, path)
24
25        # Backtrack
26        path.pop()
27
28    dfs(root, target_sum, [])
29    return result
Pattern 7.3: Binary Tree Paths (LC 257)
1def binary_tree_paths(root):
2    """
3    Find all root-to-leaf paths as strings
4    Time: O(n), Space: O(h)
5    """
6    if not root:
7        return []
8
9    result = []
10
11    def dfs(node, path):
12        if not node:
13            return
14
15        # Add current node to path string
16        path += str(node.val)
17
18        # Leaf node - add complete path
19        if not node.left and not node.right:
20            result.append(path)
21            return
22
23        # Continue path with arrow
24        path += "->"
25        dfs(node.left, path)
26        dfs(node.right, path)
27
28    dfs(root, "")
29    return result
Pattern 7.4: Sum Root to Leaf Numbers (LC 129)
1def sum_numbers(root):
2    """
3    Sum all numbers formed by root-to-leaf paths
4    Example: 1->2->3 represents 123
5    Time: O(n), Space: O(h)
6    """
7    def dfs(node, current_sum):
8        if not node:
9            return 0
10
11        # Build number: current_sum * 10 + node.val
12        current_sum = current_sum * 10 + node.val
13
14        # Leaf node - return the number
15        if not node.left and not node.right:
16            return current_sum
17
18        # Sum from both subtrees
19        return dfs(node.left, current_sum) + dfs(node.right, current_sum)
20
21    return dfs(root, 0)
Pattern 7.5: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum (LC 124)
1def max_path_sum(root):
2    """
3    Find maximum path sum (any node to any node)
4    Uses global variable to track maximum
5    Time: O(n), Space: O(h)
6    """
7    max_sum = float('-inf')
8
9    def dfs(node):
10        nonlocal max_sum
11
12        if not node:
13            return 0
14
15        # Get max contribution from left and right
16        # Use max(0, ...) to ignore negative paths
17        left_max = max(0, dfs(node.left))
18        right_max = max(0, dfs(node.right))
19
20        # Update global max with path through current node
21        path_sum = node.val + left_max + right_max
22        max_sum = max(max_sum, path_sum)
23
24        # Return max path going through this node (one side only)
25        return node.val + max(left_max, right_max)
26
27    dfs(root)
28    return max_sum
Pattern 7.6: Binary Tree Longest Consecutive Sequence (LC 298)
1def longest_consecutive(root):
2    """
3    Find length of longest consecutive path
4    Values must increase by 1 each step
5    Time: O(n), Space: O(h)
6    """
7    max_length = 0
8
9    def dfs(node, parent_val, length):
10        nonlocal max_length
11
12        if not node:
13            return
14
15        # Check if consecutive with parent
16        if node.val == parent_val + 1:
17            length += 1
18        else:
19            length = 1  # Reset sequence
20
21        # Update global max
22        max_length = max(max_length, length)
23
24        # Recurse on children
25        dfs(node.left, node.val, length)
26        dfs(node.right, node.val, length)
27
28    dfs(root, float('-inf'), 0)
29    return max_length
Pattern 7.7: Path Sum III (LC 437)
1def path_sum_3(root, target_sum):
2    """
3    Count paths with given sum (not necessarily root-to-leaf)
4    Uses prefix sum technique with hash map
5    Time: O(n), Space: O(n)
6    """
7    from collections import defaultdict
8
9    def dfs(node, current_sum, prefix_sums):
10        if not node:
11            return 0
12
13        # Update current sum
14        current_sum += node.val
15
16        # Count paths ending at current node
17        # If (current_sum - target_sum) exists, we found path(s)
18        count = prefix_sums[current_sum - target_sum]
19
20        # Add current sum to prefix map
21        prefix_sums[current_sum] += 1
22
23        # Recurse on children
24        count += dfs(node.left, current_sum, prefix_sums)
25        count += dfs(node.right, current_sum, prefix_sums)
26
27        # Backtrack: remove current sum from map
28        prefix_sums[current_sum] -= 1
29
30        return count
31
32    # Initialize with 0 sum (for paths starting from root)
33    prefix_sums = defaultdict(int)
34    prefix_sums[0] = 1
35
36    return dfs(root, 0, prefix_sums)

Java Implementations

1// Pattern 7.1: Simple Path Sum (LC 112)
2public boolean hasPathSum(TreeNode root, int targetSum) {
3    if (root == null) return false;
4
5    // Leaf node check
6    if (root.left == null && root.right == null) {
7        return root.val == targetSum;
8    }
9
10    int remaining = targetSum - root.val;
11    return hasPathSum(root.left, remaining) ||
12           hasPathSum(root.right, remaining);
13}
14
15// Pattern 7.2: Path Sum II (LC 113)
16public List<List<Integer>> pathSum(TreeNode root, int targetSum) {
17    List<List<Integer>> result = new ArrayList<>();
18    dfs(root, targetSum, new ArrayList<>(), result);
19    return result;
20}
21
22private void dfs(TreeNode node, int remaining,
23                 List<Integer> path, List<List<Integer>> result) {
24    if (node == null) return;
25
26    path.add(node.val);
27
28    if (node.left == null && node.right == null && remaining == node.val) {
29        result.add(new ArrayList<>(path));
30    }
31
32    int newRemaining = remaining - node.val;
33    dfs(node.left, newRemaining, path, result);
34    dfs(node.right, newRemaining, path, result);
35
36    path.remove(path.size() - 1);  // Backtrack
37}
38
39// Pattern 7.5: Maximum Path Sum (LC 124)
40private int maxSum = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
41
42public int maxPathSum(TreeNode root) {
43    dfs(root);
44    return maxSum;
45}
46
47private int dfs(TreeNode node) {
48    if (node == null) return 0;
49
50    int leftMax = Math.max(0, dfs(node.left));
51    int rightMax = Math.max(0, dfs(node.right));
52
53    maxSum = Math.max(maxSum, node.val + leftMax + rightMax);
54
55    return node.val + Math.max(leftMax, rightMax);
56}
57
58// Pattern 7.7: Path Sum III (LC 437)
59public int pathSum(TreeNode root, int targetSum) {
60    Map<Long, Integer> prefixSums = new HashMap<>();
61    prefixSums.put(0L, 1);
62    return dfs(root, 0L, targetSum, prefixSums);
63}
64
65private int dfs(TreeNode node, long currentSum, int target,
66                Map<Long, Integer> prefixSums) {
67    if (node == null) return 0;
68
69    currentSum += node.val;
70    int count = prefixSums.getOrDefault(currentSum - target, 0);
71
72    prefixSums.put(currentSum, prefixSums.getOrDefault(currentSum, 0) + 1);
73
74    count += dfs(node.left, currentSum, target, prefixSums);
75    count += dfs(node.right, currentSum, target, prefixSums);
76
77    prefixSums.put(currentSum, prefixSums.get(currentSum) - 1);
78
79    return count;
80}

Path Pattern Summary Table

Problem Type Approach Key Technique Time Space LC #
Simple Path Sum DFS recursion Reduce sum O(n) O(h) 112
All Paths DFS + backtrack Track path O(n) O(h) 113
Path Strings DFS + string Concatenate O(n) O(h) 257
Sum Numbers DFS + accumulate Build number O(n) O(h) 129
Max Path Sum DFS + global Track max O(n) O(h) 124
Consecutive DFS + counter Track length O(n) O(h) 298
Prefix Sum DFS + hashmap Prefix technique O(n) O(n) 437

Key Concepts & Principles

  1. Root-to-Leaf Paths

    • Always check for leaf nodes: not node.left and not node.right
    • Reduce target sum at each level
    • Return result at leaf nodes

  2. Backtracking Pattern

    • Add current node to path
    • Recurse on children
    • Remove current node from path (restore state)
    • Essential for finding all paths

  3. Global State

    • Use nonlocal/class variable for maximum values
    • Update during traversal
    • Return contribution, not final answer

  4. Path Through Node

    • For max path: left_max + node.val + right_max
    • For return: node.val + max(left_max, right_max)
    • Use max(0, …) to ignore negative contributions

  5. Prefix Sum Technique

    • Track cumulative sum from root
    • Use hashmap: prefixSum[currentSum - target] = count
    • Backtrack by decrementing counts

Common Mistakes & Pitfalls

🚫 Mistake 1: Not Checking Leaf Nodes

1# BAD: Doesn't verify it's a leaf
2if root.val == target:
3    return True
4
5# GOOD: Check both children are None
6if not root.left and not root.right and root.val == target:
7    return True

🚫 Mistake 2: Forgetting to Backtrack

1# BAD: Path grows indefinitely
2def dfs(node, path):
3    path.append(node.val)
4    dfs(node.left, path)
5
6# GOOD: Remove after recursion
7def dfs(node, path):
8    path.append(node.val)
9    dfs(node.left, path)
10    path.pop()

🚫 Mistake 3: Shallow Copy in Results

1# BAD: All results reference same list
2result.append(path)
3
4# GOOD: Create deep copy
5result.append(path[:])  # or list(path)

🚫 Mistake 4: Wrong Max Path Logic

1# BAD: Includes both subtrees in return
2def dfs(node):
3    left = dfs(node.left)
4    right = dfs(node.right)
5    return node.val + left + right  # Wrong!
6
7# GOOD: Return one path only
8return node.val + max(left, right)

🚫 Mistake 5: Not Handling Negative Values

1# BAD: Negative paths reduce maximum
2left_max = dfs(node.left)
3
4# GOOD: Ignore negative contributions
5left_max = max(0, dfs(node.left))

Key Concepts & Principles

  1. Balanced Construction

    • Always pick middle element as root to ensure balance
    • Balanced BST has height O(log n)
    • Unbalanced can degenerate to O(n)

  2. Catalan Numbers

    • Number of unique BSTs with n nodes = nth Catalan number
    • Formula: C(n) = (2n)! / ((n+1)! × n!)
    • Recurrence: C(n) = Σ C(i) × C(n-1-i) for i from 0 to n-1

  3. Traversal Properties

    • Preorder: Can reconstruct BST uniquely (root first)
    • Inorder: Gives sorted sequence (need preorder/postorder too)
    • Postorder: Can reconstruct BST uniquely (root last)

  4. Optimization Techniques

    • Use indices instead of array slicing (saves O(n) space per call)
    • Cache results for generate all problems
    • Use iterative approaches where possible

Common Mistakes & Pitfalls

🚫 Mistake 1: Array Slicing Overhead

1# BAD: Creates new arrays O(n) space each recursion
2def build(nums):
3    mid = len(nums) // 2
4    root.left = build(nums[:mid])  # O(n) space
5
6# GOOD: Use indices
7def build(left, right):
8    mid = (left + right) // 2
9    root.left = build(left, mid - 1)

🚫 Mistake 2: Off-by-One Errors

1# BAD: Wrong boundary
2mid = len(nums) // 2
3root.left = build(nums[:mid-1])  # Skips element!
4
5# GOOD: Correct boundaries
6root.left = build(nums[:mid])  # Includes all left elements

🚫 Mistake 3: Not Checking Bounds in Preorder

1# BAD: No bounds checking
2def build():
3    val = preorder[idx]
4    root = TreeNode(val)  # May violate BST property!
5
6# GOOD: Check min/max bounds
7def build(min_val, max_val):
8    if val < min_val or val > max_val:
9        return None

🚫 Mistake 4: Wrong Catalan Recurrence

1# BAD: Wrong combination
2for i in range(1, n+1):
3    dp[n] += dp[i] + dp[n-i]  # Should multiply!
4
5# GOOD: Multiply left and right counts
6for i in range(1, n+1):
7    dp[n] += dp[i-1] * dp[n-i]

🚫 Mistake 5: Wrong Middle Calculation

1# BAD: Can overflow in Java/C++
2mid = (left + right) / 2
3
4# GOOD: Prevent overflow
5mid = left + (right - left) // 2
