Complete Problem List by Difficulty

Easy Problems (Foundation)

  • LC 700: Search in a Binary Search Tree - Basic BST search
  • LC 270: Closest Binary Search Tree Value - Modified search
  • LC 108: Convert Sorted Array to BST - Basic construction
  • LC 235: Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST - Use BST property
  • LC 653: Two Sum IV - Input is a BST - Two pointers on tree
  • LC 530: Minimum Absolute Difference in BST - Inorder property
  • LC 783: Minimum Distance Between BST Nodes - Inorder traversal
  • LC 897: Increasing Order Search Tree - Inorder rebuilding
  • LC 938: Range Sum of BST - DFS with pruning
  • LC 501: Find Mode in Binary Search Tree - Inorder + counting
  • LC 112: Path Sum - Basic DFS path sum
  • LC 257: Binary Tree Paths - DFS path tracking

Medium Problems (Core)

  • LC 98: Validate Binary Search Tree - Classic validation
  • LC 173: Binary Search Tree Iterator - Design pattern
  • LC 230: Kth Smallest Element in a BST - Inorder application
  • LC 450: Delete Node in a BST - Complex restructuring
  • LC 701: Insert into a BST - Basic modification
  • LC 285: Inorder Successor in BST - BST navigation
  • LC 96: Unique Binary Search Trees - Catalan numbers
  • LC 95: Unique Binary Search Trees II - Generate all trees
  • LC 109: Convert Sorted List to BST - List to tree
  • LC 449: Serialize and Deserialize BST - Encoding/decoding
  • LC 538: Convert BST to Greater Tree - Reverse inorder
  • LC 669: Trim a Binary Search Tree - Recursive trimming
  • LC 776: Split BST - Advanced manipulation
  • LC 333: Largest BST Subtree - Subtree validation
  • LC 1008: Construct BST from Preorder - Stack approach
  • LC 1038: Binary Search Tree to Greater Sum Tree - Accumulation
  • LC 1382: Balance a Binary Search Tree - Inorder + rebuild balanced BST
  • LC 426: Convert BST to Sorted Doubly Linked List - In-place conversion
  • LC 113: Path Sum II - All root-to-leaf paths with target sum
  • LC 129: Sum Root to Leaf Numbers - DFS accumulation
  • LC 298: Binary Tree Longest Consecutive Sequence - Track sequence length
  • LC 437: Path Sum III - Any path with target sum (prefix sum)

Hard Problems (Advanced)

  • LC 99: Recover Binary Search Tree - Fix swapped nodes (see Pattern 8 below)
  • LC 1373: Maximum Sum BST in Binary Tree - Complex validation
  • LC 124: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Node-to-node max path

Template 8: Recover/Fix BST Problems

Pattern Overview

  • Description: Detect and fix violations in BST by leveraging in-order traversal property
  • Recognition: “Recover”, “fix”, “swapped nodes”, “invalid BST”
  • Key Insight: In-order traversal of valid BST = strictly increasing sequence
  • Time Complexity: O(n)
  • Space Complexity: O(h) for recursion, O(1) with Morris traversal

Why In-Order Traversal? ⭐

1Core Insight:
2  Valid BST in-order traversal → strictly INCREASING sequence
3
4  Example valid BST:        In-order: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
5         4                            ↑  ↑  ↑  ↑  ↑  ↑  ↑
6        / \                          strictly increasing ✓
7       2   6
8      / \ / \
9     1  3 5  7
10
11  If two nodes SWAPPED:     In-order: [1, 6, 3, 4, 5, 2, 7]
12         4                                 ↓        ↓
13        / \                            DROP here  DROP here
14       6   2    (swapped!)                 ↓        ↓
15      / \ / \                         first=6   second=2
16     1  3 5  7
17
18Finding "drops" in sequence = finding swapped nodes!

Two Cases of Swapped Nodes

1Case 1: ADJACENT nodes swapped (1 drop)
2  Valid:   [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
3  Swapped: [1, 3, 2, 4, 5]  ← swap 2 and 3
4                ↓
5           one drop: 3 > 2
6           first = 3 (prev at drop)
7           second = 2 (current at drop)
8
9Case 2: DISTANT nodes swapped (2 drops)
10  Valid:   [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
11  Swapped: [1, 6, 3, 4, 5, 2, 7]  ← swap 2 and 6
12                ↓           ↓
13           drop 1: 6 > 3    drop 2: 5 > 2
14           first = 6        second = 2 (update!)
15
16Key: first is set at FIRST drop, second is ALWAYS updated at each drop

Java Implementation

1// LC 99 Recover Binary Search Tree
2// Time: O(N), Space: O(H)
3
4/**
5 * Key variables:
6 * - first:  The first node where order is violated (the larger one in first drop)
7 * - second: The second node where order is violated (the smaller one in last drop)
8 * - prev:   The previously visited node in in-order traversal
9 */
10private TreeNode first = null;
11private TreeNode second = null;
12private TreeNode prev = new TreeNode(Integer.MIN_VALUE);
13
14public void recoverTree(TreeNode root) {
15    if (root == null) return;
16
17    // Step 1: In-order traversal to find the two swapped nodes
18    inorder(root);
19
20    // Step 2: Swap the values (not the nodes themselves!)
21    if (first != null && second != null) {
22        int temp = first.val;
23        first.val = second.val;
24        second.val = temp;
25    }
26}
27
28private void inorder(TreeNode root) {
29    if (root == null) return;
30
31    // 1. Go Left
32    inorder(root.left);
33
34    // 2. Process current node - detect violation
35    if (prev != null && root.val < prev.val) {
36        // Found a DROP in the sequence!
37        if (first == null) {
38            first = prev;    // First drop: prev is the "too large" node
39        }
40        second = root;       // Always update: current is the "too small" node
41    }
42
43    // Update prev for next comparison
44    prev = root;
45
46    // 3. Go Right
47    inorder(root.right);
48}

Python Implementation

1# LC 99 Recover Binary Search Tree
2class Solution:
3    def recoverTree(self, root: TreeNode) -> None:
4        self.first = None
5        self.second = None
6        self.prev = TreeNode(float('-inf'))
7
8        def inorder(node):
9            if not node:
10                return
11
12            # Go left
13            inorder(node.left)
14
15            # Detect violation (drop in sequence)
16            if self.prev and node.val < self.prev.val:
17                if self.first is None:
18                    self.first = self.prev  # First drop
19                self.second = node           # Always update
20
21            self.prev = node
22
23            # Go right
24            inorder(node.right)
25
26        inorder(root)
27
28        # Swap values
29        if self.first and self.second:
30            self.first.val, self.second.val = self.second.val, self.first.val

Why This Pattern Works

1The algorithm handles BOTH cases with ONE logic:
2
31. "first" is set only ONCE at the first drop
4   → This captures the "too large" node that was swapped
5
62. "second" is ALWAYS updated at every drop
7   → For adjacent swap: only 1 drop, second = the "too small" node ✓
8   → For distant swap: 2 drops, second gets overwritten to correct node ✓
9
10Example (distant swap: 2 and 6):
11  Sequence: [1, 6, 3, 4, 5, 2, 7]
12
13  At index 2 (val=3): prev=6, curr=3, 6 > 3 → DROP!
14    first = 6 (set once)
15    second = 3
16
17  At index 5 (val=2): prev=5, curr=2, 5 > 2 → DROP!
18    first = 6 (unchanged)
19    second = 2 (updated!) ← This is the correct second node
20
21  Swap 6 and 2 → BST recovered ✓

Similar Problems Using In-Order Property

Problem LC # Difficulty Key Technique Why In-Order?
Recover BST 99 Medium Detect 2 drops Find swapped nodes in sorted sequence
Validate BST 98 Medium Check increasing Verify strictly increasing sequence
Kth Smallest 230 Medium Count in-order In-order gives sorted order
BST Iterator 173 Medium Stack-based Simulate in-order traversal
Two Sum IV 653 Easy Two pointers In-order gives sorted array
Find Mode 501 Easy Track frequency Process sorted sequence
Min Diff in BST 530/783 Easy Track prev diff Adjacent elements in sorted order
Convert to Greater Tree 538/1038 Medium Reverse in-order Process in descending order

Common Mistakes

🚫 Mistake 1: Trying to swap nodes instead of values

1Why swap VALUES, not NODE OBJECTS?
2
3  Swapping node objects means rewiring parent/child pointers for BOTH nodes,
4  plus handling edge cases (root, adjacent nodes, left/right children).
5  This is extremely complex and error-prone.
6
7  Swapping values is O(1) and preserves the tree structure:
8  - All parent→child links stay the same
9  - All left/right subtree relationships stay the same
10  - Only the .val fields change — the BST property is restored
11
12  Example:
13       4                    4
14      / \    swap vals      / \
15     6   2   of 6 & 2 →   2   6    ← valid BST!
16    / \ / \               / \ / \
17   1  3 5  7             1  3 5  7
18
19  The nodes stay in the SAME positions. Only their values change.
20  Tree structure (edges) is completely untouched.

1// BAD: Swapping node references does NOTHING to the tree
2TreeNode temp = first;
3first = second;       // Only swaps local variable pointers!
4second = temp;        // The actual tree is unchanged.
5
6// GOOD: Swap the values stored inside the nodes
7int temp = first.val;
8first.val = second.val;
9second.val = temp;

🚫 Mistake 2: Only handling adjacent swaps

1// BAD: Stops after first drop
2if (prev.val > root.val) {
3    first = prev;
4    second = root;
5    return;  // Wrong! Miss the second drop for distant swaps
6}
7
8// GOOD: Always update second, handles both cases
9if (prev.val > root.val) {
10    if (first == null) first = prev;
11    second = root;  // Always update!
12}

🚫 Mistake 3: Not initializing prev correctly

1// BAD: prev starts as null, first comparison fails
2TreeNode prev = null;
3
4// GOOD: prev starts with MIN_VALUE for safe comparison
5TreeNode prev = new TreeNode(Integer.MIN_VALUE);
6// Or check: if (prev != null && root.val < prev.val)

Complexity Analysis

  • Time: O(N) — visit each node exactly once
  • Space: O(H) — recursion stack (H = tree height)
  • Follow-up O(1) Space: Use Morris Traversal (modifies tree temporarily)

Key Takeaways

11. In-order traversal of valid BST = STRICTLY INCREASING sequence
2   → This is the MOST IMPORTANT property for BST problems
3
42. Detecting violations = finding "drops" in the sequence
5   → prev.val > curr.val means we found a violation
6
73. Two-drop pattern handles both adjacent and distant swaps
8   → first: set at FIRST drop (the larger misplaced node)
9   → second: ALWAYS update (the smaller misplaced node)
10
114. Swap VALUES, not nodes
12   → Much simpler, preserves tree structure

1) General form

1-1) Basic OP

1-1-1) Add 1 to all nodes

1// java
2void plusOne(TreeNode root){
3    if (root == null){
4        return;
5    }
6    root.val += 1;
7    plusOne(root.left);
8    plusOne(root.right);
9}

1# python
2class TreeNode(object):
3    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
4        self.val = val
5        self.left = left
6        self.right = right
7
8root = TreeNode(0)
9root.left = TreeNode(1)
10root.right =  TreeNode(2)
11
12print (root.val)
13print (root.left.val)
14print (root.right.val)
15
16print("==============")
17
18def add_one(root):
19    if not root:
20        return
21    root.val += 1
22    add_one(root.left)
23    add_one(root.right)
24
25add_one(root)
26print (root.val)
27print (root.left.val)
28print (root.right.val)

1-1-2) Check if 2 BST are totally the same

1// java
2boolean isSameTree(TreeNode root1, TreeNode root2){
3    // if all null, then same
4    if (root1 == null && root2 == null){
5        return true;
6    }
7    if (root1 == null || root2 == null){
8        return false;
9    }
10    if (root1.val != root2.val){
11        return false;
12    }
13
14    return isSameTree(root1.left, root2.left) && isSameTree(root1.right, root2.right)
15}

1-1-3) Validate a BST

1// java
2boolean isValidBST(TreeNode root){
3    return isValidBST(root, null, null);
4}
5
6// help func
7boolean isValidBST(TreeNode root, TreeNode min, TreeNode max){
8    if (root == null){
9        return true;
10    }
11    if (min != null && root.val <= min.val){
12        return false;
13    }
14    if (max != null && root.val >= max.val){
15        return false;
16    }
17    return isValidBST(root.left, min, root) && isValidBST(root.right, root, max)
18}

1-1-4) Find if a number is in BST

1// java
2// V1 : general (for tree and BST)
3boolean isInBST(TreeNode root, int target){
4    if (root == null) return false;
5    if (root.val == target) return target;
6
7    return isInBST(root.left, target) || isInBST(root.right, target);
8}
9
10// V2 : optimization for BST
11boolean isInBST(TreeNode root, int target){
12    if (root == null) return false;
13    if (root.val == target) return target;
14
15    // optimize here
16    if (root.val < target){
17        return isInBST(root.right, target);
18    }
19    if (root.val > target){
20        return isInBST(root.left, target);
21    }
22}
23

1-1-5) Insert a number into BST

1// java
2TreeNode insertIntoBST(TreeNode root, int val){
3    // if null root, then just find a space and insert new value
4    if (root == null) return new TreeNode(val);
5    // if already exist, no need to insert, return directly
6    if (root.val == val) return root;
7
8    if (root.val < val){
9        root.right = insertIntoBST(root.right, val);
10    }
11    if (root.val > val){
12        root.left = insertIntoBST(root.left, val);
13    }
14    return root;
15}

1-1-6) Delete a node from BST

1// java
2// LC 450
3
4// V1-1
5// https://youtu.be/LFzAoJJt92M?feature=shared
6// https://github.com/neetcode-gh/leetcode/blob/main/java%2F0450-delete-node-in-a-bst.java
7public TreeNode minimumVal(TreeNode root) {
8    TreeNode curr = root;
9    while (curr != null && curr.left != null) {
10        curr = curr.left;
11    }
12    return curr;
13}
14
15public TreeNode deleteNode_1_1(TreeNode root, int key) {
16    if (root == null)
17        return null;
18
19    if (key > root.val) {
20        root.right = deleteNode_1_1(root.right, key);
21    } else if (key < root.val) {
22        root.left = deleteNode_1_1(root.left, key);
23    } else {
24        if (root.left == null) {
25            return root.right;
26        } else if (root.right == null) {
27            return root.left;
28        } else {
29            TreeNode minVal = minimumVal(root.right);
30            root.val = minVal.val;
31            root.right = deleteNode_1_1(root.right, minVal.val);
32        }
33    }
34    return root;
35}

1// java
2
3// pseudo java code
4TreeNode deleteNode(TreeNode root, int key){
5    if (root.val == key){
6        // delete
7    }
8    else if (root.val > key){
9        // to left sub tree
10        root.left = deleteNode(root.left, key);
11    }
12    else if (root.val < key){
13        // to right sub tree
14        root.right = deleteNode(root.right, key);
15    }
16    return root;
17}
18
19/** 
20 *   NOTE : 3 cases (algorithm book (labu) p.246)
21 * 
22 *   1) the value (to-delete value) is at the bottom (no sub left/right tree) -> delete directly
23 *   
24 *   2) there is only one left/right tree -> replace value with the sub-tree, then delete sub-tree
25 * 
26 *   3) there is BOTH left/right tree
27 *      -> approach 3-1)  find the MIN right sub-tree and replace with value, then delete MIN right sub-tree
28 *      -> approach 3-2)  find the MAX left sub-tree and replace with value, then delete MAX left sub-tree
29 */
30
31// java code
32TreeNode deleteNode(TreeNode root, int key){
33    if (root.val == key){
34        // case 1) & case 2)
35        if (root.left == null) return root.right;
36        if (root.right == null) return root.left;
37        
38        // case 3)
39        TreeNode minNode = getMin(root.right);
40        root.val = minNode.val;
41        root.right = deleteNode(root.right, key);
42    }
43    else if (root.val > key){
44        // to left sub tree
45        root.left = deleteNode(root.left, key);
46    }
47    else if (root.val < key){
48        // to right sub tree
49        root.right = deleteNode(root.right, key);
50    }
51    return root;
52}
53
54// help func
55TreeNode getMin(TreeNode node){
56    // min node is on the left of BST
57    while (node.left != null) node = node.left;
58    return node;
59}

1# python code
2
3# LC 450 Delete Node in a BST
4# V0
5# IDEA : RECURSION + BST PROPERTY
6#### 2 CASES :
7#   -> CASE 1 : root.val == key and NO right subtree 
8#                -> swap root and root.left, return root.left
9#   -> CASE 2 : root.val == key and THERE IS right subtree
10#                -> 1) go to 1st RIGHT sub tree
11#                -> 2) iterate to deepest LEFT subtree
12#                -> 3) swap root and  `deepest LEFT subtree` then return root
13class Solution(object):
14    def deleteNode(self, root, key):
15        if not root: return None
16        if root.val == key:
17            # case 1 : NO right subtree 
18            if not root.right:
19                left = root.left
20                return left
21            # case 2 : THERE IS right subtree
22            else:
23                ### NOTE : find min in "right" sub-tree
24                #           -> because BST property, we ONLY go to 1st right tree (make sure we find the min of right sub-tree)
25                #           -> then go to deepest left sub-tree
26                right = root.right
27                while right.left:
28                    right = right.left
29                root.val, right.val = right.val, root.val
30        root.left = self.deleteNode(root.left, key)
31        root.right = self.deleteNode(root.right, key)
32        return root

2) LC Example

2-1) Serialize and Deserialize BST

1# LC 449 Serialize and Deserialize BST
2# V0
3# IDEA : BFS + queue op
4class Codec:
5    def serialize(self, root):
6        if not root:
7            return '{}'
8
9        res = [root.val]
10        q = [root]
11
12        while q:
13            new_q = []
14            for i in range(len(q)):
15                tmp = q.pop(0)
16                if tmp.left:
17                    q.append(tmp.left)
18                    res.extend( [tmp.left.val] )
19                else:
20                    res.append('#')
21                if tmp.right:
22                    q.append(tmp.right)
23                    res.extend( [tmp.right.val] )
24                else:
25                    res.append('#')
26
27        while res and res[-1] == '#':
28                    res.pop()
29
30        return '{' + ','.join(map(str, res)) + '}' 
31
32
33    def deserialize(self, data):
34        if data == '{}':
35            return
36
37        nodes = [ TreeNode(x) for x in data[1:-1].split(",") ]
38        root = nodes.pop(0)
39        p = [root]
40        while p:
41            new_p = []
42            for n in p:
43                if nodes:
44                    left_node = nodes.pop(0)
45                    if left_node.val != '#':
46                        n.left = left_node
47                        new_p.append(n.left)
48                    else:
49                        n.left = None
50                if nodes:
51                    right_node = nodes.pop(0)
52                    if right_node.val != '#':
53                        n.right = right_node
54                        new_p.append(n.right)
55                    else:
56                        n.right = None
57            p = new_p 
58             
59        return root
60
61# V1
62# IDEA : same as LC 297
63# https://leetcode.com/problems/serialize-and-deserialize-bst/discuss/93283/Python-solution-using-BST-property
64class Codec:
65
66    def serialize(self, root):
67        vals = []
68        self._preorder(root, vals)
69        return ','.join(vals)
70        
71    def _preorder(self, node, vals):
72        if node:
73            vals.append(str(node.val))
74            self._preorder(node.left, vals)
75            self._preorder(node.right, vals)
76        
77    def deserialize(self, data):
78        vals = collections.deque(map(int, data.split(','))) if data else []
79        return self._build(vals, -float('inf'), float('inf'))
80
81    def _build(self, vals, minVal, maxVal):
82        if vals and minVal < vals[0] < maxVal:
83            val = vals.popleft()
84            root = TreeNode(val)
85            root.left = self._build(vals, minVal, val)
86            root.right = self._build(vals, val, maxVal)
87            return root
88        else:
89            return None

2-2) Kth Smallest Element in a BST

1# LC 230
2# V1'
3# IDEA : Approach 2: Iterative Inorder Traversal
4#        -> The above recursion could be converted into iteration, with the help of stack. This way one could speed up the solution because there is no need to build the entire inorder traversal, and one could stop after the kth element.
5# https://leetcode.com/problems/kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst/solution/
6class Solution:
7    def kthSmallest(self, root, k):
8        stack = []
9        
10        while True:
11            while root:
12                stack.append(root)
13                root = root.left
14            root = stack.pop()
15            k -= 1
16            if not k:
17                return root.val
18            root = root.right

2-3) Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree

1# LC 235 Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
2# V0
3# IDEA : RECURSION + POST ORDER TRANSVERSAL
4### NOTE : we need POST ORDER TRANSVERSAL for this problem
5#          -> left -> right -> root
6#          -> we can make sure that if p == q, then the root must be p and q's "common ancestor"
7class Solution:
8    def lowestCommonAncestor(self, root, p, q):
9        ### NOTE : we need to assign root.val, p, q to other var first (before they are changed)
10        # Value of current node or parent node.
11        parent_val = root.val
12
13        # Value of p
14        p_val = p.val
15
16        # Value of q
17        q_val = q.val
18
19        # If both p and q are greater than parent
20        if p_val > parent_val and q_val > parent_val:
21            ### NOTE : we need to `return` below func call   
22            return self.lowestCommonAncestor(root.right, p, q)
23        # If both p and q are lesser than parent
24        elif p_val < parent_val and q_val < parent_val: 
25            ### NOTE : we need to `return` below func call   
26            return self.lowestCommonAncestor(root.left, p, q)
27        # We have found the split point, i.e. the LCA node.
28        else:
29            ### NOTE : not root.val but root
30            return root
31
32# V0'
33# IDEA : RECURSION + POST ORDER TRANSVERSAL
34### NOTE : we need POST ORDER TRANSVERSAL for this problem
35#          -> left -> right -> root
36#          -> we can make sure that if p == q, then the root must be p and q's "common ancestor"
37class Solution(object):
38    def lowestCommonAncestor(self, root, p, q):
39        if not root:
40            return root
41
42        p_val = p.val
43        q_val = q.val
44
45        if root.val < p_val and root.val < q_val:
46            return self.lowestCommonAncestor(root.right, p, q)
47
48        elif root.val > p_val and root.val > q_val:
49            return self.lowestCommonAncestor(root.left, p, q)
50
51        else:
52            return root

2-4) Split BST (LC 776) - Recursive Partition Pattern

Pattern: Recursive BST Partition

Split a BST into two valid BSTs based on a target value. This is a partition problem, NOT a delete problem — we preserve all nodes but redistribute them into two trees.

Theory: Why Split ≠ Delete

Operation Goal Nodes Lost? Return Value
Delete (LC 450) Remove one node, keep one tree Yes (1 node) Single TreeNode
Split (LC 776) Separate into two trees No TreeNode[2] array

Core Idea

1Return value: TreeNode[2]
2  res[0] → BST with all values ≤ target
3  res[1] → BST with all values > target

Two cases based on root.val vs target:

1Case 1: root.val <= target
2  → root belongs to LEFT partition (res[0])
3  → But root.right may contain nodes > target
4  → So SPLIT root.right, reconnect the pieces
5
6Case 2: root.val > target
7  → root belongs to RIGHT partition (res[1])
8  → But root.left may contain nodes <= target
9  → So SPLIT root.left, reconnect the pieces

Visual Walkthrough

1Input:        target = 2
2         4
3        / \
4       2   6
5      / \ / \
6     1  3 5  7
7
8Step 1: root=4, val=4 > target=2 → root goes to RIGHT partition
9        Split root.left (the subtree rooted at 2)
10
11Step 2: root=2, val=2 <= target=2 → root goes to LEFT partition
12        Split root.right (the subtree rooted at 3)
13
14Step 3: root=3, val=3 > target=2 → root goes to RIGHT partition
15        Split root.left (null) → returns [null, null]
16        root.left = null (from split[1])
17        Return [null, 3]
18
19Back to Step 2: split of node 2's right returned [null, 3]
20        node 2's right = split[0] = null  (was 3, now detached)
21        Return [2→(left:1, right:null), 3]
22
23Back to Step 1: split of node 4's left returned [2, 3]
24        node 4's left = split[1] = 3  (reconnect!)
25        Return [2, 4→(left:3, right:6)]
26
27Result:
28  res[0] (≤ 2):     res[1] (> 2):
29       2                  4
30      /                  / \
31     1                  3   6
32                           / \
33                          5   7

Key Insight: The Reconnection

1When root.val <= target and we split root.right:
2  split[0] = nodes from right subtree that are still <= target
3  split[1] = nodes from right subtree that are > target
4
5  root.right = split[0]   ← keep the small ones attached to root
6  return [root, split[1]] ← root is left partition, split[1] is right partition
7
8When root.val > target and we split root.left:
9  split[0] = nodes from left subtree that are <= target
10  split[1] = nodes from left subtree that are > target
11
12  root.left = split[1]    ← keep the big ones attached to root
13  return [split[0], root] ← split[0] is left partition, root is right partition

Python Implementation

1# LC 776 Split BST
2class Solution(object):
3    def splitBST(self, root, V):
4        if not root:
5            return None, None
6        # root belongs to LEFT partition
7        elif root.val <= V:
8            result = self.splitBST(root.right, V)
9            root.right = result[0]  # keep <= V part
10            return root, result[1]  # [smallTree, bigTree]
11        # root belongs to RIGHT partition
12        else:
13            result = self.splitBST(root.left, V)
14            root.left = result[1]   # keep > V part
15            return result[0], root  # [smallTree, bigTree]

Java Implementation

1// LC 776 Split BST
2// Time: O(H), Space: O(H) where H = height of BST
3public TreeNode[] splitBST(TreeNode root, int target) {
4    if (root == null) {
5        return new TreeNode[]{null, null};
6    }
7
8    if (root.val <= target) {
9        // root goes to LEFT partition (<=target)
10        TreeNode[] split = splitBST(root.right, target);
11        root.right = split[0];          // reconnect small part
12        return new TreeNode[]{root, split[1]};
13    } else {
14        // root goes to RIGHT partition (>target)
15        TreeNode[] split = splitBST(root.left, target);
16        root.left = split[1];           // reconnect big part
17        return new TreeNode[]{split[0], root};
18    }
19}

Similar Problems

Problem LC # Similarity Key Difference
Split BST 776 Core pattern Partition into 2 trees by value
Delete Node in BST 450 Both modify BST structure Delete removes 1 node; split keeps all
Trim a BST 669 Both remove nodes outside range Trim discards nodes; split preserves all in 2 trees
Search in BST 700 Same left/right branching logic Search returns 1 node; split returns 2 trees
Insert into BST 701 Same recursive BST traversal Insert adds; split partitions
Merge Two BSTs - Inverse operation Split → 2 trees; Merge → 1 tree

Complexity

  • Time: O(H) — only visits nodes along one root-to-leaf path
  • Space: O(H) — recursion stack depth = tree height

2-5) Binary Search Tree Iterator

1# LC 173. Binary Search Tree Iterator
2# V0
3# IDEA : STACK + tree
4class BSTIterator(object):
5    def __init__(self, root):
6        """
7        :type root: TreeNode
8        """
9        self.stack = []
10        self.inOrder(root)
11    
12    def inOrder(self, root):
13        if not root:
14            return
15        """
16        NOTE !!! how we do inorder traversal here
17
18        irOrder : left -> root -> right
19        """
20        self.inOrder(root.left)
21        self.stack.append(root.val)
22        self.inOrder(root.right)
23    
24    def hasNext(self):
25        """
26        :rtype: bool
27        """
28        return len(self.stack) > 0
29
30    def next(self):
31        """
32        :rtype: int
33        """
34        return self.stack.pop(0)  # NOTE here

1// java
2// LC 173
3// V1
4// IDEA: STACK
5// https://leetcode.com/problems/binary-search-tree-iterator/solutions/52647/nice-comparison-and-short-solution-by-st-jcmg/
6public class BSTIterator_1 {
7
8    private TreeNode visit;
9    private Stack<TreeNode> stack;
10
11    public BSTIterator_1(TreeNode root) {
12        visit = root;
13        stack = new Stack();
14    }
15
16    public boolean hasNext() {
17        return visit != null || !stack.empty();
18    }
19
20    /**
21     * NOTE !!! next() method logic
22     */
23    public int next() {
24  /**
25   * NOTE !!!
26   *
27   *  since BST property is as below:
28   *
29   *  - For each node
30   *     - `left sub node < than current node`
31   *     - `right sub node > than current node`
32   *
33   *  so, within the loop over `left node`, we keep finding
34   *  the `smaller` node, and find the `relative smallest` node when the while loop ended
35   *  -> so the `relative smallest` node is the final element we put into stack
36   *  -> so it's also the 1st element pop from stack
37   *  -> which is the `next small` node
38   */
39  while (visit != null) {
40            stack.push(visit);
41            visit = visit.left;
42        }
43        // Stack: LIFO (last in, first out)
44        /**
45         * NOTE !!! we pop the `next small` node from stack
46         */
47        TreeNode next = stack.pop();
48        /**
49         * NOTE !!!  and set the `next small` node's right node as next node
50         */
51        visit = next.right;
52        /**
53         * NOTE !!! we return the `next small` node's right node val
54         *          because of the requirement
55         *
56         *         e.g. : int next() Moves the pointer to the right, then returns the number at the pointer.
57         */
58        return next.val;
59    }
60}

2-6) Search in a Binary Search Tree

1# LC 700 Search in a Binary Search Tree
2# V1
3# IDEA : RECURSION + BST property
4# https://leetcode.com/problems/search-in-a-binary-search-tree/solution/
5class Solution:
6    def searchBST(self, root: TreeNode, val: int) -> TreeNode:
7        if root is None or val == root.val:
8            return root
9        
10        return self.searchBST(root.left, val) if val < root.val \
11            else self.searchBST(root.right, val)
12
13# V1'
14# IDEA : ITERATION
15# https://leetcode.com/problems/search-in-a-binary-search-tree/solution/
16class Solution:
17    def searchBST(self, root: TreeNode, val: int) -> TreeNode:
18        while root is not None and root.val != val:
19            root = root.left if val < root.val else root.right
20        return root

2-7) Unique Binary Search Trees II

See also: Pattern 6.5 above for core idea, multiple approaches, and similar problems.

1# LC 95 Unique Binary Search Trees II
2# V1
3# IDEA : RECURSION (Cartesian product of left/right subtrees)
4# https://leetcode.com/problems/unique-binary-search-trees-ii/solution/
5#
6# Core idea:
7#   1. Pick i as root → left subtree from [start, i-1], right from [i+1, end]
8#   2. Cartesian product of all left × right subtrees
9#   3. Base case: start > end → return [None] (empty subtree)
10class Solution:
11    def generateTrees(self, n):
12        """
13        :type n: int
14        :rtype: List[TreeNode]
15        """
16        def generate_trees(start, end):
17            if start > end:
18                return [None,]
19
20            all_trees = []
21            for i in range(start, end + 1):  # pick up a root
22                # all possible left subtrees if i is choosen to be a root
23                left_trees = generate_trees(start, i - 1)
24
25                # all possible right subtrees if i is choosen to be a root
26                right_trees = generate_trees(i + 1, end)
27
28                # connect left and right subtrees to the root i
29                for l in left_trees:
30                    for r in right_trees:
31                        current_tree = TreeNode(i)
32                        current_tree.left = l
33                        current_tree.right = r
34                        all_trees.append(current_tree)
35
36            return all_trees
37
38        return generate_trees(1, n) if n else []

1// LC 95 — Java plain recursion
2// See UniqueBinarySearchTrees2.java for memoized, iterative DP,
3// and space-optimized DP approaches
4public List<TreeNode> generateTrees(int n) {
5    if (n == 0) return new ArrayList<>();
6    return buildTrees(1, n);
7}
8
9private List<TreeNode> buildTrees(int start, int end) {
10    List<TreeNode> allTrees = new ArrayList<>();
11    if (start > end) { allTrees.add(null); return allTrees; }
12    for (int i = start; i <= end; i++) {
13        List<TreeNode> leftSubtrees = buildTrees(start, i - 1);
14        List<TreeNode> rightSubtrees = buildTrees(i + 1, end);
15        for (TreeNode left : leftSubtrees) {
16            for (TreeNode right : rightSubtrees) {
17                TreeNode root = new TreeNode(i);
18                root.left = left;
19                root.right = right;
20                allTrees.add(root);
21            }
22        }
23    }
24    return allTrees;
25}

2-8) Insert into a Binary Search Tree

1# LC 701 Insert into a Binary Search Tree
2# VO : recursion + dfs
3class Solution(object):
4    def insertIntoBST(self, root, val):
5        if not root: 
6            return TreeNode(val)
7        if root.val < val: 
8            root.right = self.insertIntoBST(root.right, val);
9        elif root.val > val: 
10            root.left = self.insertIntoBST(root.left, val);
11        return(root)

2-9) Validate Binary Search Tree

1# LC 98 Validate Binary Search Tree
2# V0
3# IDEA : BFS
4#  -> trick : we make sure current tree and all of sub tree are valid BST
5#   -> not only compare tmp.val with tmp.left.val, tmp.right.val,
6#   -> but we need compare if tmp.left.val is SMALLER then `previous node val`
7#   -> but we need compare if tmp.right.val is BIGGER then `previous node val`
8class Solution(object):
9    def isValidBST(self, root):
10        if not root:
11            return True
12        _min = -float('inf')
13        _max = float('inf')
14        ### NOTE : we set q like below
15        q = [[root, _min, _max]]
16        while q:
17            for i in range(len(q)):
18                tmp, _min, _max = q.pop(0)
19                if tmp.left:
20                    """
21                    ### NOTE : below condition
22                    ### NOTE : we compare "tmp.left.val" with others (BEFORE we visit tmp.left)
23                    """
24                    if tmp.left.val >= tmp.val or tmp.left.val <= _min:
25                        return False
26                    ### NOTE : we append tmp.val as _max
27                    q.append([tmp.left, _min, tmp.val])
28                if tmp.right:
29                    """
30                    ### NOTE : below condition
31                    ### NOTE : we compare "tmp.right.val" with others (BEFORE we visit tmp.right)
32                    """
33                    if tmp.right.val <= tmp.val or tmp.right.val >= _max:
34                        return False
35                    ### NOTE : we append tmp.val as _min
36                    q.append([tmp.right, tmp.val, _max])
37        return True
38
39# V0''
40# IDEA: RECURSION 
41class Solution(object):
42    def isValidBST(self, root):
43        """
44        :type root: TreeNode
45        :rtype: bool
46        """
47        return self.valid(root, float('-inf'), float('inf'))
48        
49    def valid(self, root, min_, max_):
50        if not root: return True
51        if root.val >= max_ or root.val <= min_:
52            return False
53        return self.valid(root.left, min_, root.val) and self.valid(root.right, root.val, max_)

2-10) Trim a Binary Search Tree (LC 669) - Recursive Pruning Pattern

Pattern: Recursive BST Pruning

Trim a BST so all node values lie within [L, R]. This leverages BST property to prune entire subtrees efficiently.

Core Idea

1Three cases based on root.val vs [L, R] range:
2
3Case 1: root.val < L
4  → root and entire LEFT subtree are too small (all < L)
5  → DISCARD root and left subtree, return trimmed RIGHT subtree
6
7Case 2: root.val > R
8  → root and entire RIGHT subtree are too large (all > R)
9  → DISCARD root and right subtree, return trimmed LEFT subtree
10
11Case 3: L <= root.val <= R
12  → root is in range, KEEP it
13  → Recursively trim both subtrees

Visual Walkthrough

1Input:        L=1, R=3
2         3
3        / \
4       0   4
5        \
6         2
7        /
8       1
9
10Step 1: root=3, val=3 in [1,3] → KEEP, trim both subtrees
11
12Step 2: root=0, val=0 < L=1 → DISCARD 0, return trimBST(right=2)
13
14Step 3: root=2, val=2 in [1,3] → KEEP, trim both subtrees
15
16Step 4: root=1, val=1 in [1,3] → KEEP (leaf)
17
18Step 5: root=4, val=4 > R=3 → DISCARD 4, return trimBST(left=null) = null
19
20Result:
21     3
22    /
23   2
24  /
25 1

Key Insight: Why return trimBST(root.right, L, R) instead of return null?

1When root.val < L:
2
3  ❌ WRONG: return null
4     → This would throw away the ENTIRE subtree, but some right descendants
5        could still be in range [L, R]!
6
7  ✅ RIGHT: return trimBST(root.right, L, R)
8     → BST property guarantees: left subtree < root.val < right subtree
9     → Since root.val < L, ALL left children < root.val < L → all too small
10     → But right children > root.val, so SOME may be >= L (in range!)
11     → We discard root + left subtree, but continue searching right subtree
12
13  Example:  L=3, R=5
14         2          root=2 < L=3, discard 2 and left(1)
15        / \         but right subtree has 4 which is in [3,5]!
16       1   4        → return trimBST(4, 3, 5) → returns 4 ✅
17          / \
18         3   5
19
20Same logic (mirrored) for root.val > R:
21  → Discard root + right subtree (all too large)
22  → But left children may still be <= R (in range!)
23  → return trimBST(root.left, L, R)

Java Implementation

1// LC 669 Trim a Binary Search Tree
2// Time: O(n), Space: O(h) where h = height
3public TreeNode trimBST(TreeNode root, int L, int R) {
4    if (root == null) {
5        return root;
6    }
7
8    // Case 1: root too small, discard root + left subtree
9    if (root.val < L) {
10        return trimBST(root.right, L, R);
11    }
12
13    // Case 2: root too large, discard root + right subtree
14    if (root.val > R) {
15        return trimBST(root.left, L, R);
16    }
17
18    // Case 3: root in range, keep it and trim both subtrees
19    root.left = trimBST(root.left, L, R);
20    root.right = trimBST(root.right, L, R);
21
22    return root;
23}

Python Implementation

1# LC 669 Trim a Binary Search Tree
2class Solution:
3    def trimBST(self, root: TreeNode, low: int, high: int) -> TreeNode:
4        if not root:
5            return None
6
7        # root too small → discard root + left subtree
8        if root.val < low:
9            return self.trimBST(root.right, low, high)
10
11        # root too large → discard root + right subtree
12        if root.val > high:
13            return self.trimBST(root.left, low, high)
14
15        # root in range → keep it, trim both subtrees
16        root.left = self.trimBST(root.left, low, high)
17        root.right = self.trimBST(root.right, low, high)
18
19        return root

Comparison: Trim vs Split vs Delete

Operation LC # Nodes Removed? Return Value Key Difference
Trim 669 Yes (out of range) Single TreeNode Keeps nodes in [L,R], discards rest
Split 776 No TreeNode[2] Partitions into 2 trees, keeps ALL nodes
Delete 450 Yes (1 node) Single TreeNode Removes exactly 1 specific node

Complexity

  • Time: O(n) — visit each node at most once
  • Space: O(h) — recursion stack depth = tree height

Common Mistakes

🚫 Mistake 1: Forgetting to reconnect trimmed subtrees

1// BAD: Trims but doesn't reconnect
2if (root.val < L) {
3    trimBST(root.right, L, R);  // Missing return!
4}
5
6// GOOD: Return the trimmed subtree
7if (root.val < L) {
8    return trimBST(root.right, L, R);
9}

🚫 Mistake 2: Not recursing on both subtrees when root is in range

1// BAD: Only trims one side
2if (root.val >= L && root.val <= R) {
3    root.left = trimBST(root.left, L, R);
4    return root;  // Forgot to trim right!
5}
6
7// GOOD: Trim both sides
8root.left = trimBST(root.left, L, R);
9root.right = trimBST(root.right, L, R);
10return root;

Similar Problems:

  • LC 669 Trim a Binary Search Tree (this pattern)
  • LC 450 Delete Node in a BST (remove single node, more complex restructuring)
  • LC 776 Split BST (partition into two trees, keeps all nodes)
  • LC 98 Validate Binary Search Tree (same BST property reasoning)
  • LC 938 Range Sum of BST (same “skip subtree” optimization using BST property)

2-10-1) Convert BST to Greater Tree

1# LC 538

2-11) Binary Search Tree to Greater Sum Tree

1# LC 1038
2# Similar to LC 538 - Convert BST to Greater Tree
3# Uses reverse inorder traversal

Pattern Selection Strategy

1BST Problem Analysis Flowchart:
2
31. Does the problem require finding/searching a value?
4   ├── YES → Use Search Template (1)
5   │   ├── Exact match? → Basic binary search
6   │   ├── Closest value? → Track min difference
7   │   └── Range query? → Prune based on BST property
8   └── NO → Continue to 2
9
102. Does the problem require modifying the BST structure?
11   ├── YES → Check modification type
12   │   ├── Insert new node? → Use Insertion Template (2)
13   │   ├── Delete existing node? → Use Deletion Template (3)
14   │   └── Trim/Split tree? → Use modified Deletion Template
15   └── NO → Continue to 3
16
173. Does the problem use the sorted property of BST?
18   ├── YES → Use Inorder Template (5)
19   │   ├── Kth element? → Inorder with counter
20   │   ├── Convert to list? → Inorder traversal
21   │   └── Range sum? → Modified inorder
22   └── NO → Continue to 4
23
244. Does the problem require validating BST properties?
25   ├── YES → Use Validation Template (4)
26   │   ├── Entire tree? → Min/max bounds approach
27   │   └── Find largest valid subtree? → Bottom-up validation
28   └── NO → Continue to 5
29
305. Does the problem involve constructing a BST?
31   ├── YES → Use Construction Template (6)
32   │   ├── From sorted array? → Binary search approach
33   │   ├── From traversal? → Use BST properties
34   │   └── Generate all possible? → Recursive generation
35   └── NO → Continue to 6
36
376. Does the problem involve paths in the tree?
38   ├── YES → Use Path Template (7)
39   │   ├── Root-to-leaf sum? → DFS with target reduction
40   │   ├── All paths? → DFS with backtracking
41   │   ├── Max path sum? → DFS with global variable
42   │   └── Any path with sum? → Prefix sum technique
43   └── NO → Use Universal Template or reconsider
44

Decision Framework

  1. Identify BST property usage: Can you leverage left < root < right?
  2. Choose operation type: Search, Insert, Delete, Validate, or Construct
  3. Select traversal method: Inorder for sorted, others for structure
  4. Optimize with BST property: Prune unnecessary branches

Summary & Quick Reference

Complexity Quick Reference

Operation Average Case Worst Case Best Case Space
Search O(log n) O(n) O(1) O(h)
Insert O(log n) O(n) O(1) O(h)
Delete O(log n) O(n) O(1) O(h)
Validate O(n) O(n) O(n) O(h)
Inorder Traversal O(n) O(n) O(n) O(h)
Construction O(n) O(n) O(n) O(n)

Note: h = height of tree, n = number of nodes

Template Quick Reference

Template Best For Avoid When Key Pattern
Search Finding values Need all nodes if val < root.val: go left
Insertion Adding nodes Balancing needed Always insert as leaf
Deletion Removing nodes Multiple deletions Three cases handling
Validation Checking BST Simple traversal Min/max bounds
Inorder Sorted operations Random access Left-root-right
Construction Building BST Incremental updates Divide & conquer

Common Patterns & Tricks

Pattern: Inorder Gives Sorted Sequence

1def get_sorted_values(root):
2    """BST inorder traversal always gives sorted order"""
3    def inorder(node):
4        if not node:
5            return []
6        return inorder(node.left) + [node.val] + inorder(node.right)
7    return inorder(root)

Pattern: Reverse Inorder for Descending

1def convert_to_greater_tree(root):
2    """Process nodes from largest to smallest"""
3    self.sum = 0
4    def reverse_inorder(node):
5        if not node:
6            return
7        reverse_inorder(node.right)
8        self.sum += node.val
9        node.val = self.sum
10        reverse_inorder(node.left)
11    reverse_inorder(root)
12    return root

Pattern: Using BST Property for Optimization

1def range_sum_bst(root, low, high):
2    """Prune branches that can't contain values in range"""
3    if not root:
4        return 0
5    
6    # Prune left subtree if root is already too small
7    if root.val < low:
8        return range_sum_bst(root.right, low, high)
9    
10    # Prune right subtree if root is already too large
11    if root.val > high:
12        return range_sum_bst(root.left, low, high)
13    
14    # Root is in range, include it and check both subtrees
15    return (root.val + 
16            range_sum_bst(root.left, low, high) +
17            range_sum_bst(root.right, low, high))

Problem-Solving Steps

  1. Identify BST property usage: Can you use left < root < right?
  2. Choose appropriate template: Based on operation type
  3. Consider edge cases: Empty tree, single node, duplicates
  4. Optimize with pruning: Skip unnecessary subtrees
  5. Test with skewed trees: Worst case scenarios

Common Mistakes & Tips

🚫 Common Mistakes:

  • Not using BST property: Treating BST like regular binary tree
  • Forgetting inorder = sorted: Missing optimization opportunity
  • Wrong deletion handling: Not covering all three cases
  • Incorrect validation: Only checking parent-child, not entire subtree
  • Modifying while traversing: Can break BST property

✅ Best Practices:

  • Always leverage BST property: Prune search space when possible
  • Use inorder for sorted needs: Don’t sort separately
  • Handle duplicates explicitly: Decide if allowed in your BST
  • Consider tree balance: Mention O(n) worst case in interviews
  • Test with edge cases: Empty, single node, all left/right

Interview Tips

  1. Clarify BST properties: Can there be duplicates? Is it balanced?
  2. State complexity: Mention average O(log n) and worst O(n)
  3. Consider self-balancing: Mention AVL/Red-Black trees if relevant
  4. Use BST property: Show you understand the optimization
  5. Handle all cases: Especially for deletion (0, 1, 2 children)

Related Topics

  • Self-Balancing BSTs: AVL Tree, Red-Black Tree (guaranteed O(log n))
  • B-Trees: For database indexes (multiple keys per node)
  • Binary Heap: Different property (parent > children)
  • Trie: Prefix tree for strings
  • Segment Tree: Range queries and updates

Java Implementation Notes

1// Java BST Node
2class TreeNode {
3    int val;
4    TreeNode left, right;
5    TreeNode(int x) { val = x; }
6}
7
8// Iterative inorder with Stack
9Stack<TreeNode> stack = new Stack<>();
10TreeNode curr = root;
11while (curr != null || !stack.isEmpty()) {
12    while (curr != null) {
13        stack.push(curr);
14        curr = curr.left;
15    }
16    curr = stack.pop();
17    // Process curr
18    curr = curr.right;
19}

Python Implementation Notes

1# TreeNode class
2class TreeNode:
3    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
4        self.val = val
5        self.left = left
6        self.right = right
7
8# Generator for memory-efficient inorder
9def inorder_generator(root):
10    if root:
11        yield from inorder_generator(root.left)
12        yield root.val
13        yield from inorder_generator(root.right)

🚀 Quick Decision Tree: Choosing the Right BST Template

Use this flowchart to quickly identify which template to use for your BST problem:

1┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
2│ START: Analyze the BST Problem                  │
3└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘
4                   │
5                   ▼
6        ┌──────────────────────┐
7        │ What's the main task? │
8        └──────┬───────────────┘
9               │
10      ┌────────┴────────┐
11      │                  │
12      ▼                  ▼
13┌──────────┐      ┌──────────┐
14│ SEARCH?  │      │ MODIFY?  │
15└────┬─────┘      └────┬─────┘
16     │                 │
17     │                 ├─► Insert node? → Template 2 (Insertion)
18     │                 ├─► Delete node? → Template 3 (Deletion)
19     │                 └─► Trim/Balance? → Template 3 or 6.4 (Balance BST)
20     │
21     ├─► Find value? → Template 1 (Search)
22     ├─► Validate BST? → Template 4 (Validation)
23     └─► Closest value? → Template 1 with tracking
24
25      ▼
26┌─────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐
27│ TRAVERSAL?  │      │ CONSTRUCT?   │      │ PATH-BASED?  │
28└──────┬──────┘      └──────┬───────┘      └──────┬───────┘
29       │                     │                      │
30       │                     │                      ├─► Root-to-leaf sum? → Template 7.1
31       │                     │                      ├─► All paths? → Template 7.2
32       │                     │                      ├─► Max path sum? → Template 7.5
33       │                     │                      └─► Any path (prefix sum)? → Template 7.7
34       │                     │
35       │                     ├─► From sorted array? → Template 6.1
36       │                     ├─► From sorted list? → Template 6.2
37       │                     ├─► From preorder? → Template 6.3
38       │                     ├─► Balance existing BST? → Template 6.4
39       │                     ├─► Generate all unique? → Template 6.5
40       │                     └─► Count unique? → Template 6.6
41       │
42       ├─► Kth smallest? → Template 5 (Inorder)
43       ├─► Convert to list? → Template 5 (Inorder)
44       ├─► Range sum/query? → Template 1 with pruning
45       └─► Iterator? → Template 5 (Stack-based inorder)

📋 Quick Template Selection Table

If Problem Asks… Use Template Key Technique Typical LC Problems
“Find/search value” Template 1 Binary search property 700, 270, 938
“Insert into BST” Template 2 Recursive insertion 701
“Delete from BST” Template 3 Three-case handling 450, 669
“Is valid BST?” Template 4 Min/max bounds 98, 333
“Kth smallest/largest” Template 5 Inorder traversal 230, 173
“Convert sorted array” Template 6.1 Binary search middle 108
“Balance BST” Template 6.4 Inorder + rebuild 1382
“Path with target sum” Template 7.1 DFS + reduce sum 112
“All paths with sum” Template 7.2 DFS + backtracking 113
“Max path sum” Template 7.5 DFS + global max 124
“Any path with sum” Template 7.7 Prefix sum 437

🎯 Recognition Patterns

Keywords → Template Mapping:

  • “Search”, “find”, “closest” → Template 1 (Search)
  • “Insert”, “add node” → Template 2 (Insertion)
  • “Delete”, “remove”, “trim” → Template 3 (Deletion)
  • “Valid”, “validate”, “is BST” → Template 4 (Validation)
  • “Kth”, “sorted”, “inorder”, “iterator” → Template 5 (Inorder)
  • “Construct”, “build”, “convert”, “balance”, “generate” → Template 6 (Construction)
  • “Path”, “sum”, “maximum path”, “consecutive” → Template 7 (Path Problems)

⚡ Quick Decision Examples

Example 1: “Find the kth smallest element in a BST”

  • Keyword: “kth” + “smallest”
  • Answer: Template 5 (Inorder traversal gives sorted order)

Example 2: “Convert a sorted array to a height-balanced BST”

  • Keywords: “convert” + “sorted array”
  • Answer: Template 6.1 (Pick middle as root)

Example 3: “Find if there’s a root-to-leaf path with given sum”

  • Keywords: “path” + “sum”
  • Answer: Template 7.1 (Simple path sum)

Example 4: “Balance an existing BST”

  • Keywords: “balance” + “existing BST”
  • Answer: Template 6.4 (Inorder + rebuild)

Example 5: “Count paths with given sum (not necessarily root-to-leaf)”

  • Keywords: “any path” + “sum”
  • Answer: Template 7.7 (Prefix sum technique)

💡 Pro Tips for Template Selection

  1. Leverage BST Property: If problem can use left < root < right, use Templates 1-6
  2. Path Problems: Most work on any binary tree, not just BST (Template 7)
  3. Construction: Check input type (array/list/traversal) → different template variant
  4. Modification: Always return root after modification (Templates 2, 3, 6)
  5. When in Doubt: Check if inorder traversal helps (Template 5)

Must-Know Problems for Interviews: LC 98, 108, 112, 113, 124, 173, 230, 235, 450, 700, 701, 1382 Advanced Problems: LC 99, 124, 298, 333, 437, 776, 1373 Keywords: BST, binary search tree, inorder, sorted, validation, search tree, path sum, DFS, backtracking, balance, construction
